beats.text: Here's matrix A — entries two, one, zero, two.

Find the eigenvalues: subtract lambda down the diagonal. The characteristic polynomial is two-minus-lambda, squared, equals zero. Only one solution: lambda equals two, with algebraic multiplicity two.
Now find the eigenvectors. Compute A minus two-I — subtract two from each diagonal entry. Result: zero, one, zero, zero.
Solve A-minus-two-I times x equals zero. Row one demands x-two equals zero. Row two gives nothing new. So x-two is zero, x-one is free — the null space is spanned by the single vector one, zero.
Geometric multiplicity is one. Algebraic multiplicity is two. They don't match — a bracket appears flagging the gap between them.
One eigenvalue, one eigenvector — not enough to fill a basis. Matrix A cannot be diagonalized.
